critique_points.point_001: The paper provides limited comparison with existing HTML-specific architectures beyond MarkupLM, lacking discussion of other recent specialized models to better contextualize improvements.
critique_points.point_002: There is insufficient ablation on the composition of the pretraining corpus, particularly regarding the impact of including multilingual or raw HTML/web text during pretraining.
critique_points.point_003: The methodology for snippet extraction around salient HTML elements is unclear, with inadequate detail on heuristics, parameters, and ablation studies affecting reproducibility and understanding.
critique_points.point_004: The large-scale description generation dataset is auto-labeled without analysis of label noise or quality, raising concerns about dataset reliability and potential bounds on model accuracy.
critique_points.point_005: Navigation baselines are not fully comparable due to differences in training paradigms and data volumes, with unclear fairness in hyperparameter or iteration matching.
critique_points.point_006: The fine-tuning baseline without pretrained initialization is insufficiently described, lacking details on dataset size, training steps, and architecture parity, weakening claims about pretraining benefits.
critique_points.point_007: Evaluation of autonomous navigation is limited to the MiniWoB benchmark, which is stylized and simplified, reducing generalizability to real-world complex web environments.
critique_points.point_008: The paper lacks detailed reporting on computational costs and inference speeds across architectures and sizes, hindering assessment of practical deployability.

summary: This paper investigates the application of large pretrained language models to raw HTML understanding across three canonical tasks: semantic classification of HTML elements, description generation, and autonomous web navigation. By fine-tuning various transformer architectures, especially T5 encoder-decoder models, on large real and auto-labeled datasets, the authors demonstrate strong performance gains over specialized models and significant sample efficiency improvements. Despite promising results, challenges remain in handling long HTML documents, dataset noise, and generalizability beyond simplified benchmarks. The work contributes valuable datasets, benchmarks, and insights into model architecture and pretraining effects, advancing the integration of LLMs with autonomous web agents.
strengths: Comprehensive empirical evaluation of multiple LLM architectures and sizes on diverse HTML understanding tasks.
Development and open-sourcing of a large-scale auto-labeled dataset for description generation from CommonCrawl.
weaknesses: Limited comparison with other recent HTML-specific models and insufficient ablations on pretraining corpus composition.
Unclear snippet extraction methodology and lack of detailed analysis on dataset quality and navigation baseline fairness.
questions: How does pretraining on multilingual or web-specific raw HTML corpora impact model performance compared to pure natural language pretraining?
Can the proposed approach generalize to real-world, complex, and multilingual web navigation tasks beyond the simplified MiniWoB environment?
title: UNDERSTANDING HTML WITH LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
